lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market petroleum-refining firms that operate accountable processing assets and earn recurring external-customer revenue, including merchant or specialty refiners and any genuine toll-processing arrangements.
lens.excluded_activities: Integrated oil-company divisions outside the EBITDA band, terminals and pipelines, petroleum wholesalers, renewable-fuel plants classified elsewhere, captive internal units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Primarily commodity and specialty petroleum-product sales to distributors, marketers, industrial users, airlines, and other external buyers; qualifying tolling or processing fees are included where present.

a.bridge: BLS reports the broader NAICS 324 workforce rather than a wage-weighted task inventory for LMM petroleum refineries. The range maps AI exposure to planning, scheduling, laboratory interpretation, reliability analytics, inspection records, procurement, documentation, compliance, and operator decision support, while treating field operations, maintenance, sampling, emergency response, and manual verification as low-substitution; concentration in large plants may overstate digital sophistication relative to the tiny LMM population.
a.rationale: Refineries generate dense process, maintenance, laboratory, and commercial data that can support predictive maintenance, optimization, alarm triage, documentation, and administrative automation. Yet BLS identifies 18,720 petroleum pump system operators, refinery operators, and gaugers in the broader subsector in 2025, and safety-critical field work and accountable control-room decisions remain difficult to substitute.
a.change_evidence: Wage-weighted task logs from all candidate LMM refineries, split among operations, maintenance, inspection, laboratory, engineering, scheduling, safety, compliance, logistics, commercial, and administration, with existing control-system automation identified.
caveats: No six-digit LMM occupation mix or current-AI task audit was located.
Existing distributed-control and advanced-process-control systems may mean much of the obvious optimization opportunity is already automated.

rho.rationale: AI-assisted maintenance planning, document search, reporting, procurement, and decision support can be implemented within five years, but direct operational substitution must clear process-safety, cybersecurity, model-governance, management-of-change, validation, and workforce-training hurdles. OSHA's refinery program emphasizes implementation of process-safety procedures, human factors, training, mechanical integrity, and management of change, constraining autonomous deployment.
rho.change_evidence: Audited five-year deployment records from comparable small or specialty refineries showing approved use cases, pre/post labor hours, false-alarm and override rates, incidents, downtime, maintenance outcomes, and management-of-change approvals.
caveats: OSHA evidence establishes operational constraints, not an AI implementation rate.
A single process-safety incident or cybersecurity event could materially tighten adoption standards.

e.rationale: Petroleum refineries generally manufacture and sell products rather than supply a recurring outsourced service, so very few firms meet the frozen lens even if they fall in the EBITDA band. Genuine toll processing or specialty refining for external customers could qualify, but the provided count of six is margin-bridged using a paper/forest-products margin that is poorly matched to refinery economics and capital intensity.
e.change_evidence: A legal-entity and contract review of the six estimated firms identifying EBITDA, control ownership, revenue by commodity sale versus toll-processing fee, external-customer recurrence, asset condition, environmental liabilities, and independence from captive parents.
caveats: The frozen firm count is especially fragile because refinery economics and scale differ sharply from the margin bridge used.
Operable-refinery counts include facilities and ownership structures far outside the LMM lens.

s5.bridge: Gallup found 22% of US employer-business owners across industries planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. The refinery range discounts that intention rate for a tiny eligible population, specialized permitting, environmental liabilities, buyer qualification, capital needs, and the risk that an exit is an asset closure rather than a qualifying going-concern control transfer; industry consolidation could make the proxy conservative in individual cases.
s5.rationale: Refinery assets can transact, but the eligible LMM set is exceptionally small and transfers require deep technical, environmental, financial, and regulatory diligence. EIA recorded two refinery closures during 2025 and no implication that closures necessarily become transfers, highlighting the competing exit route.
s5.change_evidence: Closed ownership transfers, bankruptcies, conversions, and closures for every small and specialty US refinery over ten years, sourced from EIA facility histories, EPA permits, state regulators, PitchBook, lender records, and transaction filings.
caveats: Gallup is not industry- or size-specific and measures intentions rather than consummated transfers.
With very few eligible firms, one transaction changes the realized share sharply.

q.rationale: Refined-product prices are strongly tied to crude costs, regional balances, product cracks, specifications, and competition, leaving limited contractual insulation for labor or operating-cost savings. A technically advantaged specialty or constrained regional refinery may retain more through uptime and yield, but commodity buyers ultimately capture much of a broadly diffused productivity improvement through price competition.
q.change_evidence: Product- and customer-level margin bridges for comparable sales before and after implemented automation, separating crude-price and crack-spread movements, throughput, yield, downtime, quality premiums, contract formulas, and customer repricing.
caveats: No LMM refinery contract sample or automation pass-through study was located.
Regional logistics constraints and product specialization can make retention materially higher than commodity averages.

d5.bridge: EIA national refinery capacity and near-term petroleum-consumption forecasts proxy constant-price, constant-quality demand for output from the much smaller eligible LMM operator set. National product demand can be met by large domestic refineries, imports, biofuels, or plant conversions, so it may overstate demand for eligible small operators; regional constraints and specialty products may instead make national averages conservative.
d5.rationale: EIA reports US operable atmospheric capacity fell about 1% to 18.2 million barrels per calendar day at January 1, 2026 after two 2025 closures, while its prior forecast expected rising petroleum consumption alongside less refining. Five-year demand should remain substantial, but vehicle efficiency, electrification, biofuels, imports, further closures, and environmental policy create downside for the current service basket.
d5.change_evidence: EIA Annual Energy Outlook regional product-demand and refinery-throughput projections through 2031, combined with product-slate, customer, import-competition, permit, and utilization data for each eligible plant.
caveats: Capacity and national consumption are not direct measures of demand for an LMM refinery's current output mix.
Five-year policy, electric-vehicle adoption, crude differentials, and regional closures are unusually consequential.

o.rationale: Remaining refinery output requires an accountable operator to manage hazardous continuous processes, maintenance, mechanical integrity, laboratory release, emergency response, environmental permits, and process safety. AI and advanced controls can reduce staffing and improve decisions, but software alone cannot own physical containment, field verification, regulatory responsibility, or incident response.
o.change_evidence: Regulator-approved staffing models and operating data from highly automated refineries showing unattended-unit hours, remote-operation scope, minimum safe staffing, contractor dependence, incidents, and legal accountability.
caveats: An operator may remain required while ownership consolidates into a larger enterprise outside the lens.
The estimate separates operator necessity from employment intensity; it does not imply current staffing levels persist.

narrative.executive_view: Petroleum refining has useful AI applications in maintenance, planning, documentation, laboratory analytics, and decision support, but the frozen roll-up lens fits poorly because refineries principally manufacture commodities rather than provide outsourced services. Low labor share, extreme safety obligations, capital intensity, and a tiny estimated firm population further limit the implementable opportunity.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can organize operating procedures, interpret equipment histories, prioritize inspections, assist maintenance and turnaround planning, forecast quality, optimize schedules, and automate reporting. Safety-critical control, field work, mechanical integrity, emergency response, and release accountability must remain governed by qualified humans and formal management-of-change processes.
narrative.value_capture: Better uptime, yield, and energy use can have large dollar value, but refined-product pricing is governed mainly by commodity inputs, regional supply, and crack spreads. Savings may support margins temporarily, while competition and formula pricing transfer broadly available improvements to buyers.
narrative.firm_availability: The estimated six firms should not be treated as six eligible targets. Most refinery businesses fail the recurring outsourced-service test, and any small candidate requires detailed validation of tolling revenue, permits, environmental liabilities, capital needs, ownership, and transferability.
narrative.demand_durability: US petroleum demand remains large and regional refinery closures can tighten supply, but five-year outcomes vary by gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, asphalt, lubricants, and specialty products. Electrification, efficiency, biofuels, imports, conversions, and policy create meaningful downside without eliminating operator-required processing.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The primary risks are lens ineligibility, erroneous LMM firm-count bridging, environmental and remediation liabilities, catastrophic process hazards, volatile cracks, capital expenditure, and a transaction universe so small that population averages are unstable.
narrative.principal_driver: Dense operational and maintenance data can support valuable AI decision tools in a continuously operating asset.
narrative.principal_weakness: The industry is capital-intensive commodity manufacturing, not a recurring outsourced service, and almost no firms are likely to fit the LMM lens.

sources.title: Petroleum and Coal Products Manufacturing: NAICS 324
sources.supports: Reports 2025 broader-subsector employment including 18,720 petroleum pump system operators, refinery operators, and gaugers and 4,980 first-line production supervisors; reports 109,900 employees in June 2026.

sources.title: Petroleum Refinery Process Safety Management National Emphasis Program
sources.supports: Identifies catastrophic-release hazards in NAICS 324110 and process-safety requirements covering management of change, mechanical integrity, operating and emergency procedures, human factors, training, incident investigation, and implementation verification.

sources.title: U.S. refining capacity decreased during 2025
sources.supports: Reports 18.2 million barrels per calendar day of US operable atmospheric distillation capacity at January 1, 2026, down over 250,000 barrels per day, with 130 operable refineries after two 2025 closures.

sources.title: Refinery closures and rising consumption will reduce U.S. petroleum inventories in 2026
sources.supports: Forecasts rising US petroleum consumption alongside reduced domestic refining from two closures and lower inventories for gasoline, distillate, and jet fuel in 2026.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Fall 2024 US survey found 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while 5% planned closure and 5% had no plan.
